title: Thoughts on RACObserve
description: In-depth analysis of the performance pitfalls of ReactiveCocoa (RAC) in large projects: Visual analysis of the RACObserve circular reference principle and the performance bottleneck of multi-level KVO.
content:
[!WARNING]
This article discusses ReactiveCocoa (RAC) practices from the Objective-C era. Although the technology stack has shifted to Swift/Combine, the memory management and design pattern thinking behind it still have reference value.

After much anticipation, the RAC code was finally adopted in the new project. A long time ago, I used RAC extensively in a project without giving much thought to its side effects. Now that it's finally being used in a large project, I've found that the performance improvements brought by RAC are worth reconsidering.

I won't go into the details of RAC's internal implementation and code architecture, or the various advantages mentioned online. Here, I'll mainly discuss the problems I encountered.

There are roughly a few points:

  1. Circular reference.
  2. Multilevel KayPaths in KVO
  3. Code consumption

Circular reference

Let's look at the problem of circular references through two cases.

CASE ONE

Let's take a look.RACObserveThe implementation and why circular references are prone to occur:

graph TD
    Block["Block (implicitly retains self)"] -->|Captures| Self["self"]
    Self -->|Holds| Signal["RACSignal"]
    Signal -->|Subscribes| Block
    
    style Block fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Self fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Signal fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    
    linkStyle 0,1,2 stroke:red,stroke-width:2px;

Let's first look at the implementation of RACObserve.

#define _RACObserve(TARGET, KEYPATH) \
({ \
    __weak id target_ = (TARGET); \
    [target_ rac_valuesForKeyPath:@keypath(TARGET, KEYPATH) observer:self]; \
})

#if __clang__ && (__clang_major__ >= 8)
#define RACObserve(TARGET, KEYPATH) _RACObserve(TARGET, KEYPATH)
#else
#define RACObserve(TARGET, KEYPATH) \
({ \
    _Pragma("clang diagnostic push") \
    _Pragma("clang diagnostic ignored \"-Wreceiver-is-weak\"") \
    _RACObserve(TARGET, KEYPATH) \
    _Pragma("clang diagnostic pop") \
})
#endif

This code clearly shows that `self` after `Target` is inserted.

Let's look at the following code snippet, which is the source code provided by the official website.

// Observes self, and doesn't stop until self is deallocated.
RACSignal *selfSignal = RACObserve(self, arrayController.items);

// Observes the array controller, and stops when self _or_ the array
// controller is deallocated.
RACSignal *arrayControllerSignal = RACObserve(self.arrayController, items);

// Observes obj.arrayController, and stops when self _or_ the array
// controller is deallocated.
RACSignal *signal2 = RACObserve(obj.arrayController, items);

@weakify(self);
RACSignal *signal3 = [anotherSignal flattenMap:^(NSArrayController *arrayController) {
// Avoids a retain cycle because of RACObserve implicitly referencing
// self.
@strongify(self);
return RACObserve(arrayController, items);
}];

Official explanation

Creates a signal which observes KEYPATH on TARGET for changes.
In either case, the observation continues until TARGET or self is
deallocated. If any intermediate object is deallocated instead, it will be
assumed to have been set to nil.
Make sure to @strongify(self) when using this macro within a block! The
macro will always reference self, which can silently introduce a retain
cycle within a block. As a result, you should make sure that self is a weak
reference (e.g., created by @weakify and @strongify) before the
expression that uses RACObserve.

In short, RACObserve was used in the block of RAC, and then @strongify was not used again to perform a weak-strong transformation, which caused the Cycle to be retained.

CASE TWO

RACSubject is a bridge from non-RAC to RAC; you can refer to the Github tutorial for usage.
Here's a scenario I encountered, with the code below:

@implementation UIImageView (Demo)

- (RACSignal*)rbs_setImageWithURL:(NSURL *)url{
   return [self rbs_setImageWithURL:url placeholderImage:nil];
}

- (RACSignal*)rbs_setImageWithURL:(NSURL *)url placeholderImage:(UIImage *)placeholder{
    if (url.absoluteString.length == 0) {
        [self cancelCurrentImageLoad];
        self.image = placeholder;
        return [RACSignal empty];
    }
    RACSubject *subject = [RACSubject subject];
    if (self.frame.size.width == 0 && self.frame.size.height == 0) {
        dispatch_async(dispatch_get_main_queue(), ^{
            [self rbs_executeWithUrl:url placeholderImage:placeholder subject:subject];
        });
    }else{
        [self rbs_executeWithUrl:url placeholderImage:placeholder subject:subject];
    }
    return [subject deliverOnMainThread];
}

-(void)rbs_executeWithUrl:(NSURL*)url placeholderImage:(UIImage *)placeholder subject:(RACSubject*)subject{
    [self rbs_setImageWithURL:url placeholderImage:placeholder completed:^(UIImage * _Nullable image, NSError * _Nullable error, RBSImageCacheType cacheType, NSURL * _Nullable imageURL) {
        if(image && !error){
            [subject sendNext:image];
        }else{
            [subject sendError:error];
        }
    }];
}
@end

Did you spot the problem? If you don't understand the implementation of RACSubject or the information available online, you won't know why this part is identified as Leak by the Leak Profile.
The general idea is that the release of RACSubject will only be triggered by the protocol calls of SendNext, sendError, and sendComp, which seems correct from the code perspective.
However, the current scenario involves asynchronous operations and the object may be called again. The object that is thrown is held by the outer layer, which causes the problem that it is not actively released.
There are two ways to make the change: one is that whoever calls it is responsible for it, and the other is automatic release, which is done automatically when the ImageView category is deallocated.
Due to the specific nature of this project, I will discuss the second type here.
How is it implemented so that the ImageView category is automatically released when it is deallocated?
RAC internally performs dealloc swizz, which triggers a dedicated dispo object.
The revised approach (this also involved modifying the lifecycle and usage of weak objects, because the GCD switching call will be executed in the next main loop, and if the current object is released, there will be a problem here)

@implementation UIImageView (Demo)

- (RACSignal*)rbs_setImageWithURL:(NSURL *)url{
   return [self rbs_setImageWithURL:url placeholderImage:nil];
}

- (RACSignal*)rbs_setImageWithURL:(NSURL *)url placeholderImage:(UIImage *)placeholder{
    if (url.absoluteString.length == 0) {
        [self cancelCurrentImageLoad];
        self.image = placeholder;
        return [RACSignal empty];
    }
    RACSubject *subject = [RACSubject subject];
    if (self.frame.size.width == 0 && self.frame.size.height == 0) {
        _weak typeof(self) weakSelf = self;
        __weak typeof(subject) weakSubject = subject;
        dispatch_async(dispatch_get_main_queue(), ^{
            [weakSelf rbs_executeWithUrl:url placeholderImage:placeholder subject:weakSubject];
        });
    }else{
        [self rbs_executeWithUrl:url placeholderImage:placeholder subject:subject];
    }
    return [[subject deliverOnMainThread] takeUntil:self.rac_willDeallocSignal];
}

-(void)rbs_executeWithUrl:(NSURL*)url placeholderImage:(UIImage *)placeholder subject:(RACSubject*)subject{
    __weak typeof(subject) weakSubject = subject;
    [self rbs_setImageWithURL:url placeholderImage:placeholder completed:^(UIImage * _Nullable image, NSError * _Nullable error, RBSImageCacheType cacheType, NSURL * _Nullable imageURL) {
        if(image && !error){
            [weakSubject sendNext:image];
        }else{
            [weakSubject sendError:error];
        }
    }];
}
@end

The line `takeUntil:self.rac_willDeallocSignal` was used to resolve the leak issue.

[[subject deliverOnMainThread] takeUntil:self.rac_willDeallocSignal]

In summary, when using RACSubject, it's crucial to understand its underlying mechanisms. RACSubject generates a hot signal; its internal subscribeNext method triggers map, which then proceeds to bind. Bind internally blocks its own operations.
When RAC was designed, some operations needed to be unlocked manually, which caused many newcomers to face various difficulties in using it. Others may say that it will get better after this period, provided that there is an experienced RAC programmer in the team.

Multilevel KayPaths in KVO

Let's get straight to the point. As we all know, Cocoa's KVO only supports single-level KeyPaths, which are the current level of the Observer. Cross-level KeyPaths become more complex. RAC addresses this by solving the problem of multi-level KeyPaths; the RACKVOWrapper Category is a multi-level implementation.
The approach involves recursively accessing the next level of the KeyPath from the current level's Observe, and then continuing this process multiple times until the last level in the KeyPaths. The implemented code is 201 lines long, but the related business logic involves the contents of 8 header files.

In short, the first step is to parse the original KeyPaths path, through the runtime of the first-level KeyPath, and then the next level of KeyPath. This process is continuous. Then, the parsing of each level of rac_propertyAttributes is put into a collection data structure for storage.

Make your own wheel

Some people suggest that the problem could be solved by using the second-to-last KeyPath as the Observe and the last KeyPath as the final KeyPath in Cocoa.
However, this is not the case. For example, if each level of the KeyPaths has already been instantiated, there is no problem. But if the Observe is defined in init, and a certain level of the other KeyPaths has not been instantiated, the custom KVO will fail.
To give another example, if all levels on KeyPaths have been instantiated, the above solution will succeed. However, if a path in one of the previously constructed KeyPaths is suddenly set to nil and then re-initialized, KVO will fail again.
The above solutions will not work.

Code consumption

The RAC codebase is indeed quite large, with its packaging including C and ObjC, as well as locks for various scenarios.
The code for RAC's various business scenario layers comprises 533KB of code across 190 files. Even for a single scenario, this accounts for a significant portion of the operations.
Let's talk about locks first. As a commonly used business function in projects, it is also used in the main thread. RAC brings a lot of lock scenarios, covering most common locks.

The RACKVOWrapper class mentioned above involves some RAC signal-related code, and each layer of KeyPaths parsing is another layer of overhead.

There are many business scenarios involved, so I won't list them all here. Therefore, considering only one aspect is impractical. We must address each specific business need individually; there's no need to use a sledgehammer to crack a nut.

Follow-up

Our business logic uses RAC, which hinders our critical path performance. We've tried replacing RACObserve with KVO and blocks, but this blocks the implementation of multi-level KVO. We're considering writing our own multi-level KeyPaths solution, keeping it lightweight to address the immediate business needs for refactoring. Going forward, we'll prioritize event-driven implementations over excessive KVO, as KVO is like an iceberg in the ocean – too much is unsafe and unstable. RAC's concept is good, but it can be implemented in other ways. Without business needs, there's no technology.
